infobox.key: National Order of the Legion of Honour
infobox.value: ([French](/wiki/French_language "French language"): *Ordre national de la Légion d'honneur* [[ɔʁdʁ nɑsjɔnal d(ə) la leʒjɔ̃ dɔnœʁ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/French "Help:IPA/French") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:LL-Q150_(fra)-J%C3%A9r%C3%A9my-G%C3%BCnther-Heinz_J%C3%A4hnick-ordre_national_de_la_L%C3%A9gion_d%27honneur.wav "File:LL-Q150 (fra)-Jérémy-Günther-Heinz Jähnick-ordre national de la Légion d'honneur.wav")), formerly the **Imperial Order of the Legion of Honour** (*Ordre impérial de la Légion d'honneur*), is the highest and most prestigious French national [order of merit](/wiki/Order_of_merit "Order of merit"), both [military](/wiki/Military "Military") and [civil](/wiki/Civil_society "Civil society"). It consists of five classes and was originally established in 1802 by [Napoleon Bonaparte](/wiki/Napoleon "Napoleon"). The order has been retained, with occasional minor alterations, by all subsequent French governments and regimes.[[2]](#cite_note-t685-2)

("Honour and Fatherland"); its [seat](/wiki/Seat_(legal_entity) "Seat (legal entity)") is the [Palais de la Légion d'Honneur](/wiki/Palais_de_la_L%C3%A9gion_d%27Honneur "Palais de la Légion d'Honneur") next to the [Musée d'Orsay](/wiki/Mus%C3%A9e_d%27Orsay "Musée d'Orsay"), on the left bank of the [Seine](/wiki/Seine "Seine") in [Paris](/wiki/Paris "Paris").[[a]](#cite_note-3) Since 1 February 2023, the Order's grand chancellor has been retired general [François Lecointre](/wiki/Fran%C3%A7ois_Lecointre "François Lecointre"), who succeeded fellow retired general [Benoît Puga](/wiki/Beno%C3%AEt_Puga "Benoît Puga") in office.

The **National Order of the Legion of Honour** ([French](/wiki/French_language "French language"): *Ordre national de la Légion d'honneur* [[ɔʁdʁ nɑsjɔnal d(ə) la leʒjɔ̃ dɔnœʁ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/French "Help:IPA/French") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:LL-Q150_(fra)-J%C3%A9r%C3%A9my-G%C3%BCnther-Heinz_J%C3%A4hnick-ordre_national_de_la_L%C3%A9gion_d%27honneur.wav "File:LL-Q150 (fra)-Jérémy-Günther-Heinz Jähnick-ordre national de la Légion d'honneur.wav")), formerly the **Imperial Order of the Legion of Honour** (*Ordre impérial de la Légion d'honneur*), is the highest and most prestigious French national [order of merit](/wiki/Order_of_merit "Order of merit"), both [military](/wiki/Military "Military") and [civil](/wiki/Civil_society "Civil society"). It consists of five classes and was originally established in 1802 by [Napoleon Bonaparte](/wiki/Napoleon "Napoleon"). The order has been retained, with occasional minor alterations, by all subsequent French governments and regimes.[[2]](#cite_note-t685-2)

The order's [motto](/wiki/Motto "Motto") is **Honneur et Patrie** ("Honour and Fatherland"); its [seat](/wiki/Seat_(legal_entity) "Seat (legal entity)") is the [Palais de la Légion d'Honneur](/wiki/Palais_de_la_L%C3%A9gion_d%27Honneur "Palais de la Légion d'Honneur") next to the [Musée d'Orsay](/wiki/Mus%C3%A9e_d%27Orsay "Musée d'Orsay"), on the left bank of the [Seine](/wiki/Seine "Seine") in [Paris](/wiki/Paris "Paris").[[a]](#cite_note-3) Since 1 February 2023, the Order's grand chancellor has been retired general [François Lecointre](/wiki/Fran%C3%A7ois_Lecointre "François Lecointre"), who succeeded fellow retired general [Benoît Puga](/wiki/Beno%C3%AEt_Puga "Benoît Puga") in office.

The order is divided into five degrees of increasing distinction: **Chevalier** ([Knight](/wiki/Knight "Knight")), **Officier** (Officer), **Commandeur** ([Commander](/wiki/Commander_(order) "Commander (order)")), **Grand officier** (Grand Officer) and **Grand-croix** ([Grand Cross](/wiki/Grand_Cross "Grand Cross")).

### Consulate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Legion_of_Honour&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Consulate")]

During the [French Revolution](/wiki/French_Revolution "French Revolution"), all of the French [orders of chivalry](/wiki/Order_of_chivalry "Order of chivalry") were abolished and replaced with [weapons of honour](/wiki/Weapons_of_Honour "Weapons of Honour"). It was the wish of [Napoleon Bonaparte](/wiki/Napoleon "Napoleon"), the [First Consul](/wiki/French_Consulate "French Consulate"), to create a reward to commend civilians and soldiers. From this wish was instituted a *Légion d'honneur*,[[3]](#cite_note-4) a body of men that was not an order of [chivalry](/wiki/Chivalry "Chivalry"), for Napoleon believed that France wanted a [recognition of merit](/wiki/Order_of_merit "Order of merit") rather than a new system of nobility. However, the *Légion d'honneur* did use the organization of the old French orders of chivalry, for example, the *[Ordre de Saint-Louis](/wiki/Order_of_Saint_Louis "Order of Saint Louis")*. The insignia of the *Légion d'honneur* bear a resemblance to those of the *Ordre de Saint-Louis*, which also used a red ribbon.[[4]](#cite_note-5)

Napoleon originally created this award to ensure political loyalty. The organization would be used as a façade to give political favours, gifts, and concessions.[[5]](#cite_note-6) The *Légion d'honneur* was loosely patterned after a [Roman legion](/wiki/Roman_legion "Roman legion"), with [legionaries](/wiki/Legionary "Legionary"), [officers](/wiki/Officer_(armed_forces) "Officer (armed forces)"), commanders, regional "[cohorts](/wiki/Cohort_(military_unit) "Cohort (military unit)")" and a grand council. The highest rank was not a Grand Cross but a *Grand aigle* (Grand Eagle), a rank that wore the insignia common to a Grand Cross. The members were paid, the highest of them extremely generously:

* 5,000 [francs](/wiki/Franc "Franc") to a *grand officier*,
* 2,000 francs to a *commandeur*,
* 1,000 francs to an *officier*,
* 250 francs to a *légionnaire*.

Napoleon famously declared, "You call these baubles, well, it is with baubles that men are led... Do you think that you would be able to make men fight by reasoning? Never. That is good only for the scholar in his study. The soldier needs glory, distinctions, rewards."[[6]](#cite_note-7) This has been often quoted as "It is with such baubles that men are led." Napoleon was also occasionally noted after a battle to ask who the bravest man in a regiment was, and upon the regiment declaring the individual, the Emperor would take the *Legion d'honneur* from his own coat and pin it on the chest of the man.[[7]](#cite_note-8)

The order was one of the first modern [orders of merit](/wiki/Order_of_merit "Order of merit"). Under the monarchy, such orders were often limited to Roman Catholics, all [knights](/wiki/Knights "Knights") had to be noblemen, and military decorations were restricted to [officers](/wiki/Commissioned_officer "Commissioned officer").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The *Légion d'honneur*, however, was open to men of all ranks and professions; only merit or bravery counted. The only similar modern order, open to everyone, to predate it was the Swedish [Order of Vasa](/wiki/Order_of_Vasa "Order of Vasa").[[8]](#cite_note-9) The new legionnaire had to be sworn into the *Légion d'honneur*. All previous orders were Christian, or shared a clear Christian background, whereas the *Légion d'honneur* is a secular institution. The badge of the *Légion d'honneur* has five arms.

### First Empire

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Legion_of_Honour&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: First Empire")]

In a decree issued on the *10 [Pluviôse](/wiki/Pluvi%C3%B4se "Pluviôse") [XIII](/wiki/French_Republican_Calendar "French Republican Calendar")* (30 January 1805), a grand decoration was instituted. This decoration, a cross on a large [sash](/wiki/Sash "Sash") and a silver star with an eagle, symbol of the Napoleonic Empire, became known as the *Grand aigle* (Grand Eagle), and later in 1814 as the *Grand cordon* (big sash, literally "big ribbon"). After Napoleon crowned himself [Emperor of the French](/wiki/Emperor_of_the_French "Emperor of the French") in 1804 and established the [Napoleonic nobility](/wiki/Nobility_of_the_First_French_Empire "Nobility of the First French Empire") in 1808, award of the *Légion d'honneur* gave right to the title of "Knight of the Empire" (*Chevalier de l'Empire*). The title was made hereditary after three generations of grantees.

Napoleon had dispensed 15 golden collars of the *Légion d'honneur* among his family and his senior ministers. This collar was abolished in 1815.

Although research is made difficult by the loss of the archives, it is rumoured that three women who fought with the army were decorated with the order: [Virginie Ghesquière](/wiki/Virginie_Ghesqui%C3%A8re "Virginie Ghesquière"), [Marie-Jeanne Schelling](/wiki/Marie_Schellinck "Marie Schellinck") and a [nun](/wiki/Nun "Nun"), Sister [Anne Biget](/wiki/Anne_Biget "Anne Biget").[[b]](#cite_note-10)

The *Légion d'honneur* was prominent and visible in the French Empire. The Emperor always wore it, and the fashion of the time allowed for decorations to be worn most of the time. The king of [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden "Sweden") therefore declined the order; it was too common in his eyes. Napoleon's own decorations were captured by the [Prussians](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Prussia "Kingdom of Prussia") and were displayed in the *Zeughaus* (armoury) in Berlin until 1945. Today, they are in Moscow.

### Restoration of the Bourbon King of France in 1814

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Legion_of_Honour&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Restoration of the Bourbon King of France in 1814")]

Please help [improve this section](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Legion_of_Honour "Special:EditPage/Legion of Honour") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners"). Unsourced material may be challenged and [removed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Burden_of_evidence "Wikipedia:Verifiability"). *(August 2022)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

[Louis XVIII](/wiki/Louis_XVIII "Louis XVIII") changed the appearance of the order, but it was not abolished. To have done so would have angered the 35,000 to 38,000 members. The images of Napoleon and his eagle were removed and replaced by the image of King [Henry IV](/wiki/Henry_IV_of_France "Henry IV of France"), the popular first king of the [Bourbon](/wiki/House_of_Bourbon "House of Bourbon") line. Three Bourbon *[fleurs-de-lys](/wiki/Fleur-de-lys "Fleur-de-lys")* replaced the eagle on the reverse of the order. A king's crown replaced the imperial crown. In 1816, the grand cordons were renamed grand crosses and the legionnaires became knights. The king decreed that the commandants were now commanders. The *Légion d'honneur* became the second-ranking order of knighthood of the French monarchy, after the [Order of the Holy Spirit](/wiki/Order_of_the_Holy_Spirit "Order of the Holy Spirit").

### July Monarchy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Legion_of_Honour&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: July Monarchy")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8f/Louis_philippe_1835_par_desnos.jpeg/250px-Louis_philippe_1835_par_desnos.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Louis_philippe_1835_par_desnos.jpeg)

[Louis Philippe I](/wiki/Louis_Philippe_I "Louis Philippe I"), King of the French, wearing the sash and star of the Grand Cross, as well as the Officers Cross of the order

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1e/Jean-Fran%C3%A7ois_Ars%C3%A8ne_Klobb.jpg/250px-Jean-Fran%C3%A7ois_Ars%C3%A8ne_Klobb.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jean-Fran%C3%A7ois_Ars%C3%A8ne_Klobb.jpg)

[Jean-François Klobb](/wiki/Jean-Fran%C3%A7ois_Klobb "Jean-François Klobb") recipient of the Legion of Honour

Following the overthrow of the Bourbons in favour of King [Louis Philippe I](/wiki/Louis_Philippe_I "Louis Philippe I") of the [House of Orléans](/wiki/House_of_Orl%C3%A9ans "House of Orléans"), the Bourbon monarchy's orders were once again abolished and the *Légion d'honneur* was restored in 1830 as the paramount decoration of the French nation. The insignia were drastically altered; the cross now displayed [tricolour flags](/wiki/Flag_of_France "Flag of France"). In 1847, there were 47,000 members.

### Second Republic

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Legion_of_Honour&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Second Republic")]

Yet another revolution in Paris (in 1848) brought a new republic (the second) and a new design to the *Légion d'honneur*. A nephew of the founder, [Louis-Napoléon Bonaparte](/wiki/Napoleon_III "Napoleon III"), was elected president and he restored the image of his uncle on the crosses of the order. In 1852, the first recorded woman, [Angélique Duchemin](/wiki/Ang%C3%A9lique_Br%C3%BBlon "Angélique Brûlon"), an old revolutionary of the 1789 uprising against the absolute monarchy, was admitted into the order. On 2 December 1851, President Louis-Napoléon Bonaparte staged a [coup d'état](/wiki/Coup_d%27%C3%A9tat "Coup d'état") with the help of the armed forces. He made himself [Emperor of the French](/wiki/Emperor_of_the_French "Emperor of the French") exactly one year later on 2 December 1852, after a successful [plebiscite](/wiki/Plebiscite "Plebiscite").

### Second Empire

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Legion_of_Honour&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Second Empire")]

An Imperial crown was added. During [Napoleon III](/wiki/Napoleon_III "Napoleon III")'s reign, the first American was admitted: [Thomas Wiltberger Evans](/wiki/Thomas_Wiltberger_Evans "Thomas Wiltberger Evans"), dentist of Napoleon III.

### Third Republic

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Legion_of_Honour&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Third Republic")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9d/111-SC-38214_-_NARA_-_55235582-cropped.jpg/250px-111-SC-38214_-_NARA_-_55235582-cropped.jpg)](/wiki/File:111-SC-38214_-_NARA_-_55235582-cropped.jpg)

[Philippe Pétain](/wiki/Philippe_P%C3%A9tain "Philippe Pétain") and [John J. Pershing](/wiki/John_J._Pershing "John J. Pershing") were decorated with the *Grand-croix* of the Legion of Honor, as were several US generals with the *Commandeur* and *Chevalier* medal shortly after World War I, in 1919.

In 1870, the defeat of the French Imperial Army in the [Franco-Prussian War](/wiki/Franco-Prussian_War "Franco-Prussian War") brought the end of the Empire and the creation of the [Third Republic](/wiki/French_Third_Republic "French Third Republic") (1871–1940). As France changed, the *Légion d'honneur* changed as well. The crown was replaced by a [laurel](/wiki/Laurel_wreath "Laurel wreath") and oak wreath. In 1871, during the [Paris Commune](/wiki/Paris_Commune "Paris Commune") uprising, the *[Hôtel de Salm](/wiki/Palais_de_la_L%C3%A9gion_d%27Honneur "Palais de la Légion d'Honneur")*, headquarters of the *Légion d'honneur*, was burned to the ground in fierce street combats; the archives of the order were lost.

In the second term of President [Jules Grévy](/wiki/Jules_Gr%C3%A9vy "Jules Grévy"), which started in 1885, newspaper journalists brought to light the trafficking of Grévy's son-in-law, Daniel Wilson, in the awarding of decorations of the *Légion d'honneur*. Grévy was not accused of personal participation in this scandal, but he was slow to accept his indirect political responsibility, which caused his eventual resignation on 2 December 1887.

During [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"), some 55,000 decorations were conferred, 20,000 of which went to foreigners. The large number of decorations resulted from the new posthumous awards authorised in 1918. Traditionally, membership in the *Légion d'honneur* could not be awarded posthumously.

### Fourth and Fifth Republics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Legion_of_Honour&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Fourth and Fifth Republics")]

The establishment of the [Fourth Republic](/wiki/French_Fourth_Republic "French Fourth Republic") in 1946 brought about the latest change in the design of the Legion of Honour. The date "1870" on the obverse was replaced by a single star. No changes were made after the establishment of the [Fifth Republic](/wiki/French_Fifth_Republic "French Fifth Republic") in 1958.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/53/Legion_d%E2%80%99Honneur_Evolution.png/1280px-Legion_d%E2%80%99Honneur_Evolution.png)](/wiki/File:Legion_d%E2%80%99Honneur_Evolution.png)

Evolution of the insignia of the Grand Cross of the Legion d'Honneur through Frances various governments and regimes, from the First Empire to the current Fifth Republic

### Legal status and leadership

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Legion_of_Honour&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Legal status and leadership")]

The Legion of Honour is a national order of France, meaning a public incorporated body. The Legion is regulated by a [civil law code](/wiki/Code_(law) "Code (law)"), the "Code of the Legion of Honour and of the Military Medal". While the [President of the French Republic](/wiki/President_of_France "President of France") is the Grand Master of the order, day-to-day running is entrusted to the Grand Chancery (**Grande Chancellerie de la Légion d'honneur**).

#### Grand Master

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Legion_of_Honour&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Grand Master")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e9/GrandCollier.jpg/250px-GrandCollier.jpg)](/wiki/File:GrandCollier.jpg)

Current [collar](/wiki/Collar_(order) "Collar (order)") of the Fifth Republic, which is the insignia of the [Grand Master](/wiki/President_of_France "President of France") at the [Élysée Palace](/wiki/%C3%89lys%C3%A9e_Palace "Élysée Palace") during a presidential inauguration. The collar is usually kept in the [Musée de la Legion d'Honneur](/wiki/Mus%C3%A9e_de_la_L%C3%A9gion_d%27honneur "Musée de la Légion d'honneur").

Since the establishment of the Legion, the Grand Master of the order has always been the Emperor, King or President of France. [President](/wiki/President_of_France "President of France") [Emmanuel Macron](/wiki/Emmanuel_Macron "Emmanuel Macron") therefore became the Grand Master of the Legion upon his inauguration as President on 14 May 2017.[[9]](#cite_note-11)

The Grand Master appoints all other members of the order, on the advice of the French government. The Grand Master's insignia is the [Grand Collar](/wiki/Grand_Collar "Grand Collar") of the Legion. The President of the Republic, as Grand Master of the order, receives the Collar as part of his investiture, but the Grand Masters have not worn the Collar since [Valéry Giscard d'Estaing](/wiki/Val%C3%A9ry_Giscard_d%27Estaing "Valéry Giscard d'Estaing").[[10]](#cite_note-France24-12)

#### The Grand Chancery

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Legion_of_Honour&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: The Grand Chancery")]

The Grand Chancery is headed by the Grand Chancellor (*grand chancelier*), usually a retired general, as well as the Secretary-General (*secrétaire général*), a civilian administrator.

* Grand Chancellor: [General](/wiki/Army_General_(France) "Army General (France)") [François Lecointre](/wiki/Fran%C3%A7ois_Lecointre "François Lecointre") (since 2023)
* Secretary-General: Julien Le Gars (since 2023)[[11]](#cite_note-13)

The Grand Chancery also regulates the [National Order of Merit](/wiki/National_Order_of_Merit_(France) "National Order of Merit (France)") and the *[médaille militaire](/wiki/M%C3%A9daille_militaire "Médaille militaire")* (Military Medal). There are several structures funded by and operated under the authority of the Grand Chancery, like the [Legion of Honour Schools](/wiki/Maison_d%27%C3%A9ducation_de_la_L%C3%A9gion_d%27honneur "Maison d'éducation de la Légion d'honneur") (*Maisons d'éducation de la Légion d'honneur*) and the [Legion of Honour Museum](/wiki/Mus%C3%A9e_de_la_L%C3%A9gion_d%27honneur "Musée de la Légion d'honneur") (*Musée de la Légion d'honneur*). The Legion of Honour Schools are élite boarding schools in [Saint-Denis](/wiki/Saint-Denis,_Seine-Saint-Denis "Saint-Denis, Seine-Saint-Denis") and Camp des Loges in the forest of [Saint-Germain-en-Laye](/wiki/Saint-Germain-en-Laye "Saint-Germain-en-Laye"). Study there is restricted to daughters, granddaughters, and great-granddaughters of members of the order, the *[médaille militaire](/wiki/M%C3%A9daille_militaire "Médaille militaire")* or the *[ordre national du Mérite](/wiki/Ordre_national_du_M%C3%A9rite "Ordre national du Mérite")*.[[12]](#cite_note-14)

### Membership

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Legion_of_Honour&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Membership")]

See also: [Lists of Légion d'honneur recipients](/wiki/Lists_of_L%C3%A9gion_d%27honneur_recipients "Lists of Légion d'honneur recipients")

There are five classes in the Legion of Honour:

* ***Chevalier*** (Knight): minimum 20 years of public service or 25 years of professional activity with "eminent merits"
* ***Officier*** (Officer): minimum 8 years in the rank of *Chevalier*
* ***Commandeur*** (Commander): minimum 5 years in the rank of *Officier*
* ***Grand officier*** (Grand Officer): minimum 3 years in the rank of *Commandeur*
* ***Grand-croix*** (Grand Cross): minimum 3 years in the rank of *Grand-officier*

The "eminent merits" required to be awarded the order require the flawless performance of one's trade as well as doing more than ordinarily expected, such as being creative, zealous and contributing to the growth and well-being of others.

The order has a maximum quota of 75 Grand Cross, 250 Grand Officers, 1,250 Commanders, 10,000 Officers, and 113,425 (ordinary) Knights. As of 2010,[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Legion_of_Honour&action=edit) the actual membership was 67 Grand Cross, 314 Grand Officers, 3,009 Commanders, 17,032 Officers and 74,384 Knights. Appointments of veterans of [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), French military personnel involved in the [North African Campaign](/wiki/North_African_Campaign "North African Campaign") and other foreign French military operations, as well as wounded soldiers, are made independently of the quota.

Members convicted of a felony (*crime* in French) are automatically dismissed from the order. Members convicted of a misdemeanour (*délit* in French) can be dismissed as well, although this is not automatic.

Wearing the decoration of the *Légion d'honneur* without having the right to do so is a serious offence. Wearing the ribbon or [rosette](/wiki/Rosette_(decoration) "Rosette (decoration)") of a foreign order is prohibited if that ribbon is mainly red, like the ribbon of the Legion of Honour.

In theory, French military personnel in uniform are required to salute other military members in uniform wearing the medal (but not the ribbon), whatever the *Légion d'honneur* rank and the military rank of the bearer. In practice, however, this is rarely done.

There is not a single, complete list of all the members of the Legion in chronological order. The number is estimated at one million, including about 2,900 Knights Grand Cross.[[13]](#cite_note-15)

#### French nationals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Legion_of_Honour&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: French nationals")]

French nationals, men and women, can be received into the *Légion*, for "eminent merit" (*mérites éminents*) in military or civil life. In practice, in current usage, the order is conferred on entrepreneurs, high-level [civil servants](/wiki/French_Civil_Service "French Civil Service"), scientists, artists, including famous actors and actresses, sport [champions](/wiki/Champion "Champion"),[[c]](#cite_note-16) and others with connections in the [executive](/wiki/Executive_branch "Executive branch"). Members of the [French Parliament](/wiki/French_Parliament "French Parliament") cannot receive the order, except for valour in war,[[14]](#cite_note-17) and ministers are not allowed to nominate their accountants.

Until 2008, French nationals could only enter the Legion of Honour at the class of *Chevalier* (Knight). To be promoted to a higher class, one had to perform new eminent services in the interest of France and a set number of years had to pass between appointment and promotion. This was however amended in 2008 when entry became possible at Officer, Commander and Grand Officer levels, as a recognition of "extraordinary careers" (*carrières hors du commun*). In 2009, [Simone Veil](/wiki/Simone_Veil "Simone Veil") became the first person to enter the Order at Grand Officer level.[[15]](#cite_note-18) Veil was a member of the *[Académie française](/wiki/Acad%C3%A9mie_fran%C3%A7aise "Académie française")*, a former [Health Minister](/wiki/Minister_of_Health_(France) "Minister of Health (France)") and [President of the European Parliament](/wiki/President_of_the_European_Parliament "President of the European Parliament"), as well as an [Auschwitz](/wiki/Auschwitz "Auschwitz") survivor. She was promoted to Grand Cross in 2012.

Every year at least five recipients decline the award. Even if they refuse to accept it, they are still included in the order's official membership.[[10]](#cite_note-France24-12) The composers [Maurice Ravel](/wiki/Maurice_Ravel "Maurice Ravel") and [Charles Koechlin](/wiki/Charles_Koechlin "Charles Koechlin"), for example, declined the award when it was offered to them.[[16]](#cite_note-grove-19)[[17]](#cite_note-20)

#### Non-French recipients

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Legion_of_Honour&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Non-French recipients")]

Main articles: [List of foreign recipients of the Légion d'Honneur by country](/wiki/List_of_foreign_recipients_of_the_L%C3%A9gion_d%27Honneur_by_country "List of foreign recipients of the Légion d'Honneur by country") and [List of foreign recipients of the Légion d'Honneur by decade](/wiki/List_of_foreign_recipients_of_the_L%C3%A9gion_d%27Honneur_by_decade "List of foreign recipients of the Légion d'Honneur by decade")

While membership in the *Légion* is technically restricted to French nationals,[[18]](#cite_note-21) foreign nationals who have served France or the ideals it upholds[[19]](#cite_note-22) may receive the honour.[[20]](#cite_note-23) Foreign nationals who live in France are subject to the same requirements as the French. Foreign nationals who live abroad may be awarded a distinction of any rank or dignity in the *Légion*. Foreign heads of state and their spouses or consorts of monarchs are made Grand Cross as a courtesy. American and British veterans who served in either [World War](/wiki/World_War "World War") on French soil,[[21]](#cite_note-24) or during the 1944 campaigns to liberate France,[[22]](#cite_note-25)[[23]](#cite_note-26) may be eligible for appointment as Chevalier of the Legion of Honour, provided they were still living when the honour was approved.

#### Collective awards

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Legion_of_Honour&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Collective awards")]

Collective appointments can be made to cities, institutions or companies. A total of 64 settlements in France have been decorated, as well as six foreign cities: [Liège](/wiki/Li%C3%A8ge "Liège") in 1914,[[24]](#cite_note-27) [Belgrade](/wiki/Belgrade "Belgrade") in 1920,[[25]](#cite_note-28) [Luxembourg City](/wiki/Luxembourg_City "Luxembourg City") in 1957, [Volgograd](/wiki/Volgograd "Volgograd") (the World War II 'Stalingrad') in 1984,[[26]](#cite_note-29) [Algiers](/wiki/Algiers "Algiers") in 2004, and [London](/wiki/London "London") in 2020.[[27]](#cite_note-30)[[28]](#cite_note-31) French towns display the decoration in their [municipal coat of arms](/wiki/Civic_heraldry "Civic heraldry").

Organisations to receive the honour include the [French Red Cross](/wiki/French_Red_Cross "French Red Cross") (*Croix-Rouge Française*), the *Abbaye de Nôtre-Dame des Dombes* ([Abbey of Notre-Dame des Dombes](/wiki/Abbey_of_Notre-Dame_des_Dombes "Abbey of Notre-Dame des Dombes")), the French National Railway Company (*[SNCF](/wiki/SNCF "SNCF"), Société Nationale des Chemins de fer Français*), the *Préfecture de Police de la Ville de Paris* ([Prefecture of Police of Paris](/wiki/Prefecture_of_Police_of_Paris "Prefecture of Police of Paris")), and various *[Grandes Écoles](/wiki/Grandes_%C3%89coles "Grandes Écoles")* (National (Elite) Colleges) and other educational establishments.

#### Military awards

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Legion_of_Honour&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Military awards")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7b/Ordre_national_de_la_L%C3%A9gion_d%E2%80%99Honneur%2C_Croix_de_Chevalier_attribu%C3%A9e_au_lieutenant_Tessier.jpg/250px-Ordre_national_de_la_L%C3%A9gion_d%E2%80%99Honneur%2C_Croix_de_Chevalier_attribu%C3%A9e_au_lieutenant_Tessier.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ordre_national_de_la_L%C3%A9gion_d%E2%80%99Honneur,_Croix_de_Chevalier_attribu%C3%A9e_au_lieutenant_Tessier.jpg)

Notice of posthumous award of the *Croix de Chevalier* to Lieutenant Robert Léon Tessier—*[Mort pour la France](/wiki/Mort_pour_la_France "Mort pour la France")* ("Died for France") in [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I")

The military distinctions (*Légion d'honneur à titre militaire*) are awarded for bravery (*actions de guerre*) or for service.

* award for extreme bravery: the *Légion d'Honneur* is awarded jointly with a [mention in dispatches](/wiki/Mention_in_dispatches "Mention in dispatches"). This is the top valour award in France. It is rarely awarded, mainly to soldiers who have died in battle.
* award for service: the *Légion* is awarded without any citation.

##### French service-members

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Legion_of_Honour&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: French service-members")]

For active-duty commissioned officers, the Legion of Honour award for service is achieved after 20 years of meritorious service, having been awarded the rank of *Chevalier* of the *[Ordre National du Mérite](/wiki/Ordre_National_du_M%C3%A9rite "Ordre National du Mérite")*. Bravery awards lessen the time needed for the award—in fact decorated servicemen become directly chevaliers of the *Légion d'Honneur*, skipping the *Ordre du Mérite*. Non-commissioned officers (NCOs) almost never achieve that award, except for the most heavily decorated service members.

##### Collective military awards

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Legion_of_Honour&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Collective military awards")]

Collective appointments can be made to military units. In the case of a military unit, its flag is decorated with the insignia of a knight, which is a different award from the *[fourragère](/wiki/Fourrag%C3%A8re "Fourragère")*. Twenty-one schools, mainly schools providing reserve officers during the World Wars, were awarded the Légion d'Honneur. Foreign military units can be decorated with the order, such as the [U.S. Military Academy](/wiki/U.S._Military_Academy "U.S. Military Academy").

## Classes and insignia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Legion_of_Honour&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Classes and insignia")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0a/Wearing_of_the_insignia_of_the_L%C3%A9gion_d%27honneur_%28gentlemens%29.svg/960px-Wearing_of_the_insignia_of_the_L%C3%A9gion_d%27honneur_%28gentlemens%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Wearing_of_the_insignia_of_the_L%C3%A9gion_d%27honneur_(gentlemens).svg)

The five classes wearing their respective insignia (gentlemen): 1: *Chevalier*; 2: *Officier*; 3: *Commandeur*; 4: *Grand-officier*; 5: *Grand-croix*

The order has had five levels since the reign of King Louis XVIII, who restored the order in 1815. Since the reform, the following distinctions have existed:

* Three ranks:
  + *Chevalier* (Knight): badge worn on left breast suspended from ribbon
  + *Officier* (Officer): badge worn on left breast suspended from a ribbon with a [rosette](/wiki/Rosette_(decoration) "Rosette (decoration)")
  + *Commandeur* (Commander): badge around neck suspended from ribbon necklet
* Two dignities:
  + *Grand officier* (Grand Officer): badge worn on left breast suspended from a ribbon (Officer), with star displayed on right breast
  + *Grand-croix* (Grand Cross), formerly *Grande décoration*, *Grand aigle*, or *Grand cordon*: the highest level; badge affixed to sash worn over the right shoulder, with a gilt silver star displayed on left breast

Due to the order's long history, and the remarkable fact that it has been retained by all subsequent governments and regimes since the First Empire, the order's design has undergone many changes. Although the basic shape and structure of the insignia has remained generally the same, the hanging device changed back and forth and France itself swung back and forth between [republic](/wiki/Republic "Republic") and [monarchy](/wiki/Monarchy "Monarchy"). The central disc in the centre has also changed to reflect the political system and leadership of France at the time. As each new regime came along the design was altered to become politically correct for the time, sometimes even changed multiple times during one historical era.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b6/Design_of_the_Legion_d%E2%80%99Honneur_through_the_years.png/1280px-Design_of_the_Legion_d%E2%80%99Honneur_through_the_years.png)](/wiki/File:Design_of_the_Legion_d%E2%80%99Honneur_through_the_years.png)

How the design of the *Légion d'honneur* changed through each various era and regime, shown through the example of the obverse and reverse of the Officers cross. See below for a detailed explanation of each era.

The *badge* of the *Légion* is shaped as a five-armed "Maltese [Asterisk](/wiki/Asterisk "Asterisk")", using five distinctive "arrowhead" shaped arms inspired by the [Maltese Cross](/wiki/Maltese_Cross_(symbol) "Maltese Cross (symbol)"). The badge is rendered in [gilt](/wiki/Gilding "Gilding") (in silver for chevalier) enameled white, with an enameled [laurel](/wiki/Laurel_wreath "Laurel wreath") and [oak](/wiki/Oak "Oak") wreath between the arms. The obverse central disc is in gilt, featuring the head of [Marianne](/wiki/Marianne "Marianne"), surrounded by the legend *République Française* on a blue enamel ring. The reverse central disc is also in gilt, with a set of crossed *[tricolores](/wiki/Flag_of_France "Flag of France")*, surrounded by the Légion's motto *Honneur et Patrie* ('Honour and Fatherland') and its foundation date on a blue enamel ring. The badge is suspended by an enameled laurel and oak wreath.

The *star* (or *plaque*) is worn by the Grand Cross (in gilt on the left chest) and the Grand Officer (in silver on the right chest) respectively; it is similar to the badge, but without enamel, and with the wreath replaced by a cluster of rays in between each arm. The central disc features the head of Marianne, surrounded by the legend *République Française* ('French Republic') and the motto *Honneur et Patrie*.[[29]](#cite_note-33)

The *[ribbon](/wiki/Ribbon "Ribbon")* for the medal is plain red.

The badge or star is not usually worn, except at the time of the decoration ceremony or on a [dress uniform](/wiki/Dress_uniform "Dress uniform") or [formal wear](/wiki/Formal_wear "Formal wear"). Instead, one normally wears the ribbon or rosette on their suit.

For less formal occasions, recipients wear a simple stripe of thread sewn onto the lapel (red for *chevaliers* and *officiers*, silver for *commandeurs*). Except when wearing a dark suit with a lapel, women instead typically wear a small lapel pin called a barrette. Recipients purchase the special thread and barrettes at a store in Paris near the [Palais-Royal](/wiki/Palais-Royal "Palais-Royal").[[30]](#cite_note-34)

| Historical Era/Period | Notes | Obverse | Reverse | Hanging Device |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| [1804](/wiki/French_First_Republic "French First Republic") | The first model of the Legion d'Honneur did not hang from a crown or wreath. It lasted for just 9 months from May 1804 until February 1805 and encompassed the founding of the [First French Empire](/wiki/First_French_Empire "First French Empire") on the 18th May 1804. Despite being officially established on 19 May 1802, no awards were made until this version. This version shows the [Emperor](/wiki/Emperor_of_the_French "Emperor of the French") on the obverse and the imperial eagle on the reverse. The text on the ring on both the obverse and reverse would remain the same during the entirety of [Napoleon](/wiki/Napoleon "Napoleon")'s reign. |  |  |  |
| [1805](/wiki/First_French_Empire "First French Empire") | The second model of differed only from the first by the addition of the [imperial crown](/wiki/Crown_of_Napoleon "Crown of Napoleon") atop the cross, and was attached to one of the arms of the cross. The image of the Emperor is also slightly smaller than the previous version, while the reverse ring also has a stylised wreath at the bottom instead of three stars. |  |  |  |
| [1806–1808](/wiki/First_French_Empire "First French Empire") | The third model is very similar to the previous second version however the depiction of Napoleon is more similar to the first version and the obverse ring has a wreath at the bottom. The crown, while almost identical to that of the second version this time is free-hanging and separately fixed above the cross. |  |  |  |
| [1808–1809](/wiki/First_French_Empire "First French Empire") | The fourth model has as slightly different depiction of the Emperor while the obverse ring has a star and dots in place of the previous versions wreath. The reverse of the fourth model is notable as its the only First Empire model with the eagle facing to the right, while the bottom of the ring has three stars reminiscent of the first model. The crown the cross hangs from is also very different compared to the previous two versions. |  |  |  |
| [1809–1814](/wiki/First_French_Empire "First French Empire") | The fifth and final version of the First Empire is different from the other versions by the execution of larger text on the rings, with the reverse showing a distinct wreath like object at the bottom. The obverse on some models shows and enamelled [laurel wreath](/wiki/Laurel_wreath "Laurel wreath") adorning the Emperors head, while on the reverse the eagle is back facing left. The crown is also radically different from the previous models. |  |  |  |
| [1814–1830](/wiki/Bourbon_Restoration_in_France "Bourbon Restoration in France") | The sixth model from the [Bourbon Restoration](/wiki/Bourbon_Restoration_in_France "Bourbon Restoration in France") period marks the first major alteration from the original design, due to the fact that the [regime](/wiki/Kingdom_of_France "Kingdom of France") and leader of France had changed. The crown the cross hangs from has been altered and also features the main symbol of the [House of Bourbon](/wiki/House_of_Bourbon "House of Bourbon"); the [fleur-de-lis](/wiki/Fleur-de-lis "Fleur-de-lis"). The obverse features the profile of "The Good King" [Henri IV](/wiki/Henry_IV_of_France "Henry IV of France") with the text of the ring bearing the words; Henry IV, King of France and the [Navarre](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Navarre "Kingdom of Navarre"). The reverse keeps the text of the previous versions; *Honneur et Patrie* and depicts the three fleurs-de-lis, the symbol of the Bourbons. |  |  |  |
| [1830–1848](/wiki/July_Monarchy "July Monarchy") | The seventh model from the [July Monarchy](/wiki/July_Monarchy "July Monarchy") period is similar to the previous Bourbon Restoration period. The crown is very similar, with just the fleur-de-lis omitted, the obverse keeps the profile of Henri IV but the obverse ring bears just his name, with the rest of the ring filled with stars and a wreath. The reverse bears the first depiction of what would continue for many future iterations; the two crossed [tricolours](/wiki/Tricolour "Tricolour") with the usual reverse ring motto *Honneur et Patrie*. |  |  |  |
| [1848–1851](/wiki/French_Second_Republic "French Second Republic") | The eighth model, used for only three years during the [Second Republic](/wiki/French_Second_Republic "French Second Republic") is the only other example apart from the first model to not have any hanging device (no crown/wreath). The obverse once again shows a portrait of Napoleon, with the text reading "Bonaparte [First Consul](/wiki/French_Consulate "French Consulate")" and the date of the order's founding; 19 May 1802. The reverse shows the crossed tricolours as before, however this time the *Honneur et Patrie* is written underneath and not on the ring, the first and only time this was the case. The reverse ring instead reads *République Française* which would later feature on the obverse ring. |  |  |  |
| [1851–1852](/wiki/French_Second_Republic "French Second Republic") | The ninth or *[La Presidence](/wiki/Napoleon_III "Napoleon III")* model was only used between 1851 and 1852 and is considered by some to be a hybrid model. It is at the very least a transitional model from the design used during the Second Republic to the [Second Empire](/wiki/Second_French_Empire "Second French Empire"). The execution of the cross is very similar to Second Republic models, just with the addition of a crown (different to that of the Second Empire models) while the obverse continues to show Napoleon, with the ring text of *Napoleon Emp. des Français*. The reverse shows the imperial eagle and the usual ring text. The central discs bear a striking resemblance to the fifth model. |  |  |  |
| [1852–1870](/wiki/Second_French_Empire "Second French Empire") | The tenth model used in the Second Empire would be the last to date to use either Napoleon's image or a crown of any sort. The crown used is quite unique and resembles the [Crown of Napoleon III](/wiki/Crown_of_Napoleon_III "Crown of Napoleon III"), while the obverse shows the Napoleon I with the ring text of *Napoleon Empereur des Français* (the only model to fully spell out Emperor). The reverse shows the usual imperial eagle, though this time facing right like the fourth model. The usual reverse ring text is present with a large wreath at the bottom. |  |  |  |
| [1870–1940](/wiki/French_Third_Republic "French Third Republic") | The eleventh model created for the [Third French Republic](/wiki/French_Third_Republic "French Third Republic") would be another radical change, and the first to show much of the symbolism of today's model. It was the first model to hang from a wreath of laurel and oak leaves, and the first to feature the profile of [Marianne](/wiki/Marianne "Marianne") on the obverse. The ring on the obverse reads; *République Française*, the first since the early Second Republic and the first time on the obverse, with the date [1870](/wiki/1870_in_France "1870 in France"). The back features the tricolours and the usual text of *Honneur et Patrie*, in a design almost identical to the seventh model used during the July Monarchy. |  |  |  |
| [1946–](/wiki/France "France") | The 12th, final and current version is almost identical to the 11th. The only differences are found on the ring of the obverse, where the date of 1870, the Third Republic's founding, is replaced with a star. The reverse is also almost identical, with just the wreath at the bottom of the ring being replaced with *29 Floréal an X* ([29 Floréal Year 10](/wiki/French_Republican_calendar "French Republican calendar")), the date of the order's founding (19 May 1802) in the [French Revolutionary Calendar](/wiki/French_Republican_calendar "French Republican calendar"). Except for these changes the *Legion d'Honneur* has remained unchanged from 1870, with this exact form being kept during both the [Fourth](/wiki/French_Fourth_Republic "French Fourth Republic") and current [Fifth Republic](/wiki/French_Fifth_Republic "French Fifth Republic"). |  |  |  |

## References and notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Legion_of_Honour&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: References and notes")]

**Notes**

1. **[^](#cite_ref-3)** The award for the French Legion of Honour is known by many titles, also depending on the five levels of degree: Knight of the Legion of Honour; **Chevalier de la Légion d'honneur**; Officer of the Legion of Honour; **Officier de la Légion d'honneur**; Commander of the Legion of Honour; **Commandeur de la Légion d'honneur**; Grand Officer of the Legion of Honour; **Grand officier de la Légion d'honneur**; Grand Cross of the Legion of Honour; **Grand'Croix de la Légion d'honneur**. The word **honneur** is often capitalised, as in the name of the palace **[Palais de la Légion d'Honneur](/wiki/Palais_de_la_L%C3%A9gion_d%27Honneur "Palais de la Légion d'Honneur")**.
2. **[^](#cite_ref-10)** The first recorded women's award is 1851, under [Louis-Napoleon Bonaparte](/wiki/Napoleon_III "Napoleon III").
3. **[^](#cite_ref-16)** All Olympic Gold Medal winners are awarded the *Légion*.
4. **[^](#cite_ref-32)** Officially, military units are not members of the Legion of Honour, which include only individuals. As for foreign Legionnaires, they are "decorated with the Legion of Honour insignia", not "member of the Legion of Honour". Do not confuse military units that received the *[fourragère](/wiki/Fourrag%C3%A8re "Fourragère")* to the colour of the ribbon of the Legion of Honour (units quoted at six, seven or eight times in the order of the army with military units whose flag is decorated with the Cross of the Legion of Honour.
